ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai garlic black pepper crispy chicken with rice. FOODON_00002670 (chicken dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Thai-style garlic black pepper crispy chicken with rice.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for this specific Thai crispy chicken dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 150 g of crispy chicken pieces and 250 g of steamed white rice, as typically plated at a Thai restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Fried chicken (battered or breaded) and cooked white rice — composite estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter (FDC ID 171477) and Rice, white, long-grain, regular, cooked (FDC ID 169756)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA or HPB Singapore entry exists for Thai garlic black pepper crispy chicken with rice as a composite dish. Values are estimated by blending USDA SR Legacy data for batter-fried chicken (approximately 37.5% of 400 g serving = 150 g) and cooked long-grain white rice (approximately 62.5% = 250 g), then back-calculating to per-100 g. Garlic and black pepper seasoning contribute negligible macro change at typical usage levels. Sodium is elevated to reflect soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning typical of Thai restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white rice, boiled (GI 72); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: The GI of this dish is dominated by the steamed white rice component (approximately 62.5% of serving weight). White rice (boiled, long-grain) has a published GI of 72 (high) per Atkinson et al. 2008 and the University of Sydney GI Database. The crispy chicken component contributes negligible glycemic load. Composite dish GI estimated at 72 (high), consistent with rice-dominant Thai meals. Glycemic load per 400 g serving calculated as: (72 × 22.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 400 g / 100) / 100 = approximately 64.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to an estimated sodium content of approximately 380 mg per 100 g (approximately 1520 mg per standard 400 g serving), which represents a substantial portion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index driven by steamed white rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this dish, as the steamed white rice component produces a high glycemic index of approximately 72 and a glycemic load of approximately 64.5 per standard serving, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; substituting brown rice or reducing portion size is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and elevated sodium from deep-frying and seasoning sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, this dish presents moderate concern due to saturated fat from deep-frying (approximately 1.6 g per 100 g) and high sodium from soy and oyster sauces; occasional consumption in reduced portions is preferable, and grilled chicken alternatives should be considered.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Garlic Black Pepper Crispy Chicken with Steamed Rice is a Thai-inspired dish consisting of deep-fried chicken pieces coated in a seasoned batter flavoured prominently with garlic and coarsely ground black pepper, served alongside a generous portion of plain steamed white rice. The chicken is typically marinated with garlic, black pepper, soy sauce, and oyster sauce before being fried to achieve a golden, crunchy exterior while retaining juicy meat inside. The bold, pungent aroma of garlic combined with the sharp heat of black pepper gives the dish a distinctive flavour profile that is savoury, slightly spicy, and deeply aromatic. In Singapore, this dish is served at Thai casual-dining restaurants such as Saap Saap Thai and is popular as a satisfying lunch or dinner option. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate source of protein (approximately 12.8 g per 100 g) and carbohydrates (approximately 22.4 g per 100 g), with the rice component contributing the majority of the carbohydrate and glycemic load. The dish carries a high glycemic index of approximately 72 due to the white rice base, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is elevated (approximately 380 mg per 100 g) due to soy sauce and oyster sauce used in seasoning, which is a consideration for those managing hypertension or cardiovascular risk. The dish is generally halal-suitable and dairy-free but contains soy and gluten allergens from the sauces and batter.

confidence_reason: No exact match for this specific Thai garlic black pepper crispy chicken with rice dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for batter-fried chicken and cooked white rice in proportions consistent with a standard restaurant serving. GI is estimated from the published GI of white rice (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008). Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimate. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimate. All other -1 values reflect nutrients not reported in the primary source data. Nutrient values represent a composite estimate for the full dish (chicken plus rice) back-calculated to per-100 g basis. Sodium has been adjusted upward from the base USDA values to reflect typical Thai restaurant seasoning with soy sauce and oyster sauce. The GI of 72 is assigned based on the white rice component, which dominates the glycemic response of this dish, per Atkinson et al. 2008 and the University of Sydney GI Database. This dish is served at Saap Saap Thai (Funan, Singapore) and sourced from field survey data (GD001570). The cuisine_tags 'Pad Thai' and 'Thai Noodle' from the original input record are not applicable to this dish and have been excluded.
